prompt: It is a legal requirement to have a bell on your bicycle in the Netherlands.

options.label: True
False

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A bell is legally required on all bicycles in the Netherlands. It must be in working order and audible. A horn or other sound device doesn't count.
feedback.rule: Voertuigreglement Art. 5.5.7 — a working bell is compulsory.
feedback.tip: No bell = fine. Make sure yours actually rings.

prompt: What is the fine for cycling at night without front or rear lights?

options.label: €35 per missing light
€55 per missing light
€95 for both lights missing
No fine — lights are advisory at night

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The fine is €55 per missing light — so €110 if both front and rear are missing. Reflectors don't count as lights.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 35 — active lights required. €55 fine per light.
feedback.tip: €55 front, €55 rear. Both active, not just reflectors.

prompt: It is legal to cycle with both earbuds in while listening to music.

options.label: True
False

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Wearing both earbuds is illegal in the Netherlands. One ear is permitted, but two ears are considered to obstruct your ability to hear traffic. The fine is €160.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 61a — two earbuds prohibited. €160 fine.
feedback.tip: One ear in, one ear out. Two ears = €160.

prompt: You're cycling on a quiet residential street with a friend. Is it legal to ride side by side?

options.label: No — cyclists must always ride single file
Yes — two abreast is permitted
Only if you're on a cycle path

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cycling two abreast is legal in the Netherlands. You may ride side by side on any road. Three or more abreast is not permitted unless on a closed cycle path.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 — two cyclists abreast is permitted on all roads.
feedback.tip: Two abreast: legal. Three abreast: not allowed.

prompt: Cycling on the pavement (voetpad) is illegal in the Netherlands.

options.label: True
False

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cycling on a pavement marked 'voetpad' is illegal. Cyclists must use the road or a designated cycle path. The exception is when a sign explicitly permits cyclists.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 — voetpad is for pedestrians only. Cyclists excluded unless signed.
feedback.tip: Voetpad = walking only. No bike unless a sign says otherwise.

prompt: You want to carry a passenger on your bicycle. Under what condition is this allowed?

options.label: It's never allowed on a standard bike
Only children under 8 in a proper child seat
Anyone on a rear rack if you go slowly
Passengers are allowed if your bike has footrests and handlebars for them

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Carrying a passenger is legal if your bike is equipped with a proper passenger seat, footrests, and something to hold on to. Children under 8 must be in a certified child seat. Perching someone on a rack without footrests is illegal.
feedback.rule: Voertuigreglement — passenger bikes must have proper seating, footrests, and handholds.
feedback.tip: Footrests + something to hold = legal. Rack-perching = not legal.

prompt: You approach a red cyclist light on an empty junction at 2am. No cars or pedestrians anywhere. What is the legal position if you ride through?

options.label: Legal — no danger, so no offence
Legal — cyclist lights are advisory between midnight and 6am
Illegal — same €160 fine as at any other time
Legal — you may proceed if you give way first

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Running a red cyclist light is illegal at all times, regardless of traffic conditions. The €160 fine applies 24 hours a day. There are no exemptions based on time or traffic.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 68 — red lights are mandatory at all times. €160 fine.
feedback.tip: Red means stop. Always. €160 doesn't care what time it is.

prompt: You're cycling and want to tow your friend's broken bike alongside yours. You hold their handlebar with one hand. Is this legal?

options.label: Yes — as long as you go slowly
Yes — towing by hand is a common Dutch practice
No — you must have both hands available to steer and brake

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Holding another bike's handlebar while cycling is illegal because you cannot control your own bike properly. You need both hands to steer and brake safely. The broken bike needs to be fixed or transported another way.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 — cyclists must maintain full control. Both hands required.
feedback.tip: Both hands on your own bars. No handlebar-to-handlebar towing.

prompt: Your bicycle has no rear reflector. Is this a problem during the day?

options.label: Yes — reflectors are required at all times
No — reflectors are only required at night
No — only lights are required, not reflectors
Yes — but only if you're on a main road

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Reflectors are required on your bicycle at all times, not just at night. A red rear reflector, white or yellow pedal reflectors, and white front reflector are all mandatory. Lights are additional requirements after dark.
feedback.rule: Voertuigreglement — reflectors required at all times. Lights required additionally at night.
feedback.tip: Reflectors = always. Lights = additionally at night.

prompt: The 0.5‰ blood alcohol limit for cyclists only applies to riders over 18.

options.label: True
False

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The 0.5‰ limit applies to all cyclists regardless of age. In fact, riders under 24 with less than 5 years' driving experience face a stricter 0.2‰ limit. The rules are strict across the board.
feedback.rule: WVW Art. 8 — 0.5‰ for adults, 0.2‰ for younger/inexperienced road users.
feedback.tip: Under 24 and less experienced? Your limit is 0.2‰, not 0.5‰.

prompt: You see a yellow diamond sign on your road. In Dutch this is called 'voorrangsweg'. What does it mean for you?

options.label: Yield to all traffic at the next junction
You are on a priority road — crossing traffic must yield to you
Speed limit ahead
Road narrows — give way to oncoming traffic

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Voorrangsweg' means priority road. The yellow diamond sign means you have right of way at all junctions until you see an end-of-priority sign (yellow diamond with diagonal bar).
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 16 — voorrangsweg = priority road.
feedback.tip: Yellow diamond on your road = you have priority. Look for the end sign.

prompt: A sign at roadworks reads 'Pas op'. What does this mean?

options.label: Road closed
No entry
Watch out / caution
Slow down to 10km/h

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Pas op' means watch out or caution. It's a general warning. You'll see it at roadworks, near schools, and wherever attention is needed — not a specific legal obligation, but a signal to be alert.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: pas = step/watch, op = up/on. Pas op = watch out.
feedback.tip: Pas op = pay attention. Something needs your focus ahead.

prompt: A sign at a junction reads 'Fietsers oversteken'. What does it mean?

options.label: No cyclists beyond this point
Cyclists crossing ahead — watch out
Cycle path begins here
Cyclists must dismount

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Fietsers oversteken' means cyclists crossing. It appears where a cycle path crosses a road, warning approaching traffic. Knowing this sign helps you understand where cars are being alerted to expect you.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: fietsers = cyclists, oversteken = to cross.
feedback.tip: Oversteken = crossing. Fietsers oversteken = cyclists crossing here.

prompt: A sign at an intersection reads 'Doorrijden verboden'. You are in a queue of traffic. What must you do?

options.label: Continue through — 'verboden' means caution, not stop
Do not enter the intersection unless you can clear it fully
Yield to pedestrians only
Stop and wait for a green light regardless of space ahead

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Doorrijden verboden' means no entry into a box junction unless your exit is clear. 'Doorrijden' means to drive through, 'verboden' means forbidden. You must not block the intersection.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: doorrijden = to drive through, verboden = forbidden.
feedback.tip: Verboden = forbidden. Doorrijden verboden = don't block the box.

prompt: A sign reads 'Voetgangersoversteekplaats'. What is this?

options.label: A pedestrian underpass
A pedestrian crossing — zebra crossing
A pedestrian-only zone
A shared path for cyclists and pedestrians

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Voetgangersoversteekplaats' means pedestrian crossing — the zebra crossing. 'Voetgangers' means pedestrians, 'oversteekplaats' means crossing place. Cyclists must yield to pedestrians on these markings.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: voetgangers = pedestrians, oversteekplaats = crossing place.
feedback.tip: Voetgangers = walkers. Oversteekplaats = crossing. Big word, simple rule: yield.

prompt: You see a road sign with 'Inhalen verboden' below a no-overtaking symbol. You're on your bike. Does this apply to you?

options.label: No — overtaking rules don't apply to cyclists
Yes — 'inhalen verboden' applies to all road users including cyclists
Only if you're overtaking a motor vehicle
Only on roads without a cycle lane

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Inhalen verboden' means no overtaking and it applies to cyclists too unless an exception sign is shown. You cannot overtake any vehicle in the same direction, including other cyclists.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: inhalen = to overtake, verboden = forbidden. Applies to all vehicles.
feedback.tip: Inhalen verboden applies to bikes too. No overtaking means no overtaking.

prompt: A sign reads 'Verboden in te rijden' with a 'Uitgezonderd bestemmingsverkeer' sub-sign. You live on this street. Can you enter?

options.label: No — 'verboden in te rijden' means no entry for anyone
Yes — 'bestemmingsverkeer' means access for destination traffic, which includes residents
Only if you're on a bicycle
Only between 7am and 7pm

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Bestemmingsverkeer' means destination traffic — people going to an address on that street. If you live there, you are bestemmingsverkeer and may enter despite the no-entry sign.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: bestemming = destination, verkeer = traffic. Bestemmingsverkeer = access for residents and deliveries.
feedback.tip: Bestemmingsverkeer = you live here, you may enter. Destination traffic.

prompt: You're approaching an unmarked crossing. There are no traffic lights, no signs. A tram is approaching from the left. Who has priority?

options.label: You — it's an unmarked crossing, right-before-left applies
The tram — trams always have priority, with or without signs
Whoever arrives at the crossing first

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Trams always have priority, including at unmarked crossings. The right-before-left rule does not override tram priority. Trams run on fixed tracks and cannot deviate — they have absolute priority regardless of signs.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 11 — trams have priority at all times, all locations.
feedback.tip: Tram = always wins. Right-before-left doesn't apply to trams.

prompt: You're at a junction waiting at a green cyclist light. You begin to move. A tram approaches on its own green phase, crossing your path. What do you do?

options.label: Continue — you have a green light and entered the junction lawfully
Stop immediately — trams have priority over all signals
Speed up to clear the tracks before the tram arrives

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A green light does not override a tram's priority. Even when you have a green cyclist signal, a tram on its own phase has absolute right of way. Stop, let it pass, then proceed.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 11 — tram priority overrides all traffic signals.
feedback.tip: Green light ≠ tram yielded. Always scan for trams before crossing tracks.

prompt: A tram stop has no raised platform. The tram has stopped and the doors open. Passengers are stepping directly into the road in front of you. What do you do?

options.label: Ring your bell and continue — passengers should look for cyclists
Slow right down and yield — they are pedestrians stepping into the road
Ride between the tram and the passengers carefully

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Passengers stepping off a tram are pedestrians entering the road. You must yield and give them space to move safely. Riding between the tram and disembarking passengers is dangerous and not permitted.
feedback.rule: Tram passengers disembarking must be given safe passage — they are pedestrians.
feedback.tip: Tram stopped + doors open = pedestrians emerging. Yield.

prompt: You're cycling on a cycle path that crosses a zebra crossing. A pedestrian steps onto the crossing. You must yield.

options.label: True
False — cyclists on a cycle path have priority

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cyclists must yield to pedestrians on a zebra crossing, even when riding on a dedicated cycle path. The zebra marking applies to all vehicles crossing it, including cyclists.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 49a — all road users yield to pedestrians on a zebra crossing.
feedback.tip: Zebra crossing = pedestrian priority. Cycle path doesn't change that.

prompt: A pedestrian is crossing the road in the middle of the block — not at a crossing. They step in front of you. Do they have right of way?

options.label: Yes — pedestrians always have priority over cyclists
No — but you must still avoid them
No — they are at fault and you may continue

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A pedestrian not at a designated crossing does not have legal priority. However, you are still obligated to do everything possible to avoid a collision. Being legally in the right doesn't help anyone if there's an accident.
feedback.rule: Pedestrians only have legal priority at designated crossings. Cyclists must still avoid collisions everywhere.
feedback.tip: No crossing = no legal priority for them. But you still can't hit them.

prompt: You're cycling through a busy market street. Pedestrians are walking in the road alongside market stalls. There's no cycle path. What is your obligation?

options.label: Pedestrians must clear the road — cycle at normal speed
Adapt your speed to the pedestrians and pass safely — they have as much right to be there
Dismount and walk your bike
Ring your bell continuously to clear a path

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: When pedestrians are legally using a shared road space, you must adapt your speed and behaviour to pass them safely. You don't have to dismount, but you do need to slow down and give them space. Continuous bell-ringing is not a solution.
feedback.rule: Cyclists must not endanger other road users. Adapt speed to conditions.
feedback.tip: Shared space = shared respect. Slow down, pass safely.

prompt: You arrive at a roundabout that has no separate cycle ring — just a normal roundabout with one lane. How do you use it?

options.label: You join the main flow with cars and follow the same rules as a car
You have priority over all cars as a vulnerable road user
You must dismount and walk around the outside

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Without a separate cycle ring, cyclists join the main roundabout lane and follow the same rules as cars. There is no automatic cyclist priority. You yield to traffic already in the roundabout when entering.
feedback.rule: Without a cycle ring, cyclists are regular traffic in the roundabout.
feedback.tip: No cycle ring = you're a car in the roundabout. Same rules apply.

prompt: You're cycling clockwise around a roundabout and want to take the third exit. When should you signal right?

options.label: Before entering the roundabout
As you pass the first exit
As you pass the second exit (the one before yours)
Cyclists don't need to signal on roundabouts

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Signal right as you pass the exit just before the one you want. This gives cars and other cyclists at your exit time to see you're leaving. Signalling too early is confusing; signalling as you arrive at your exit is too late.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 — signal right when leaving a roundabout, before your exit.
feedback.tip: Pass the prior exit, then signal. One exit's worth of warning.

prompt: You're on a roundabout cycle ring. A pedestrian steps from the pavement onto the ring path directly in front of you. What do you do?

options.label: Continue — you have priority on the ring
Ring your bell and continue
Yield — the pedestrian is on a zebra crossing or has stepped into your path

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cyclist priority on the ring applies to cars, not pedestrians. If a pedestrian is on a zebra crossing or has stepped into your path, you must yield to them. Cyclist ring priority does not override pedestrian rights.
feedback.rule: Ring priority applies to vehicles. Pedestrians on crossings still have priority over cyclists.
feedback.tip: Ring priority = over cars. Pedestrians are a different story.

prompt: You are cycling along a road with a bus lane marked 'Lijnbus'. You may use this lane.

options.label: True
False — bus lanes are for buses only

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cyclists may generally use bus lanes in the Netherlands unless a sign specifically excludes them. 'Lijnbus' lanes are typically shared with cyclists. A separate 'uitgezonderd fietsers' sub-sign would be shown if cyclists are excluded.
feedback.rule: Bus lanes are accessible to cyclists unless a sign explicitly excludes them.
feedback.tip: Bus lane = cyclists welcome unless a sign says otherwise.

prompt: You see a 'Voetgangersgebied' sign at the entrance to a shopping street. Can you cycle through it?

options.label: Yes — cyclists are always allowed in pedestrian zones
Only if a sub-sign or painted marking explicitly permits cyclists
No — voetgangersgebied means no bikes ever

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Voetgangersgebied' is a pedestrian-only zone. Cyclists are only permitted if a sub-sign (e.g. 'uitgezonderd fietsers') or a blue cycling symbol on the road explicitly allows it. Without that, you must dismount or find another route.
feedback.rule: Voetgangersgebied = pedestrians only. Cyclists need explicit permission to enter.
feedback.tip: Pedestrian zone = look for the exception sign. No sign = no cycling.

prompt: A delivery van is parked blocking your cycle path. You ride around it into the main road and a car clips you. Who is legally at fault?

options.label: You — you chose to enter the main road
The car driver — they should have been watching
The van driver who blocked the cycle path illegally

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The illegally parked van created the hazard. By forcing you off the cycle path, the van driver bears legal responsibility for the consequences. This doesn't mean you shouldn't ride carefully — but the primary fault lies with the obstruction.
feedback.rule: Parking on a cycle path is illegal. The person creating the obstruction bears liability for resulting incidents.
feedback.tip: Illegal obstruction = liability for what follows. Document it.

prompt: You're on a roundabout cycle ring. Shark teeth face a car approaching the entry ahead. The car is about to cross your path. Who has priority?

options.label: The car — it's already in the roundabout
You — shark teeth face the car, confirming your ring priority
Whoever is moving faster

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Shark teeth facing the car confirm it must yield to you on the cycle ring. The markings override any assumption the car might have from being in the roundabout approach lane. Your ring priority is confirmed.
feedback.rule: Shark teeth facing cars at entry/exit = cyclist ring priority confirmed.
feedback.tip: Shark teeth face the yielder. Facing the car = you have priority.

prompt: You approach a crossing. Shark teeth are on your side. Another cyclist approaches from your right on the crossing road. Who goes first?

options.label: You — the other cyclist is coming from the right, not a priority road
The other cyclist — shark teeth override right-before-left
Whoever arrives at the crossing first

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The shark teeth on your side override the right-before-left rule. Even though the other cyclist is on your right — which would normally give them priority anyway — the shark teeth are the deciding factor. Markings always take precedence over default rules.
feedback.rule: Road markings override all default priority rules.
feedback.tip: Markings first, defaults second. Shark teeth = yield, regardless of direction.

prompt: You're cycling on a road marked with a yellow diamond (voorrangsweg). A car approaches from your right at an unmarked junction. Who has priority?

options.label: The car — it comes from the right
You — the priority road sign overrides right-before-left
It depends on who slows down first

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The voorrangsweg (priority road) sign means all crossing and joining traffic must yield to you, regardless of direction. Right-before-left applies at unmarked junctions between equal roads — but you are on a priority road, which overrides that entirely.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 16 — voorrangsweg overrides right-before-left.
feedback.tip: Yellow diamond = your road wins. Right-before-left doesn't apply here.

prompt: You have a green cyclist light. As you start crossing, a tram approaches from the left on its own green phase. What do you do?

options.label: Continue — you entered the junction on green
Stop — trams have priority over all signals
Speed up to clear the tracks

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Trams have absolute priority over all traffic signals. Your green light controls the junction for cars and cyclists — it does not govern tram movements. A tram on its own phase has right of way regardless of your signal.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 11 — tram priority overrides all signals.
feedback.tip: Green for you ≠ tram stopped. Scan for trams before every crossing.

prompt: A car signals to turn right across your cycle path. You have a red cyclist light. The car has green. What happens?

options.label: The car must yield to you — turning traffic always yields to straight-going cyclists
You must stop — you have a red light, and the car has green
You both stop and negotiate

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Although turning traffic normally yields to straight-going cyclists, you have a red light — meaning you are not permitted to be in that space at all. The car's green overrides the normal turning-yields rule here. Stop and wait for your green.
feedback.rule: A red cyclist light prevents entry — the turning-yields rule only applies when you have right of movement.
feedback.tip: Turning yields — but only when YOU have legal right to be there. Red = stop.

prompt: You're on a roundabout cycle ring. A car is exiting across your path. You look but see NO shark teeth on the road at this exit. Who has priority?

options.label: You — cyclists on roundabout rings always have priority
The car — without shark teeth, cyclist ring priority is not established
The car — exiting traffic always has priority

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Cyclist priority on a roundabout ring is established by shark teeth facing the cars — not assumed. Without that marking, the default right-before-left rules apply. Never assume ring priority without confirming the markings.
feedback.rule: Ring priority = shark teeth at car crossings. No markings = no priority.
feedback.tip: No teeth, no priority. Scan the road before assuming.

prompt: You're cycling on a fietsstraat (auto te gast). At an unmarked T-junction, a car approaches from your right on the joining road. Who has priority?

options.label: You — on a fietsstraat cyclists are always priority
The car — right-before-left applies even on a fietsstraat
Neither — both stop and negotiate

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Auto te gast' means cars are guests and may not overtake cyclists — but it does not override the right-before-left priority rule at junctions. At an unmarked crossing, the standard right-before-left still applies to all road users, including on a fietsstraat.
feedback.rule: Fietsstraat rules govern speed and overtaking — not junction priority.
feedback.tip: Fietsstraat ≠ priority at junctions. Right-before-left still applies.

prompt: You're overtaking a slower cyclist on a fietspad. A car pulls up behind you both and starts overtaking. What should you do?

options.label: Move aside — give the car room to pass safely
Hold your line — the car is not permitted on the fietspad at all
Stop and wait until the car has passed

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A fietspad is a mandatory cycle path — cars are prohibited from using it at all. The car overtaking you isn't just being impatient; it's in the wrong space entirely. You hold your line because you have every right to be there and the car does not.
feedback.rule: G11/G13 — fietspad is mandatory for cyclists. Motor vehicles prohibited.
feedback.tip: If a car is on the fietspad, it's in the wrong place entirely.

prompt: You're cycling on a voorrangsweg (priority road). Your road crosses a zebra crossing where a pedestrian has stepped out. Does your priority road sign protect you here?

options.label: Yes — you are on a priority road and have right of way
No — priority road signs apply to vehicles, not pedestrians. You must yield.
Only if the pedestrian stepped out without looking

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Priority road signs (voorrangsweg) govern right of way between vehicles at junctions. They do not apply to pedestrians at zebra crossings. You must always yield to pedestrians on a zebra crossing, regardless of what road you're on.
feedback.rule: Voorrangsweg applies to vehicle junctions. Zebra crossings override it for pedestrians.
feedback.tip: Priority road = beats other vehicles. Never beats a pedestrian on a zebra.

prompt: You're cycling on the horizontal bar of a T-junction. A car comes from the road joining from the right. Who has priority?

options.label: You — you're on the main through road
The car — it comes from the right
It depends on which road is wider

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: At a T-junction without signs, right-before-left applies just as at a crossroads. The car joining from the right has priority over you even though you're on the 'main' road. Road width and apparent importance don't override the rule.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 15 — right-before-left applies at T-junctions too.
feedback.tip: T-junction, crossroads, any junction — right-before-left applies the same way.

prompt: A car is reversing out of a private driveway and crosses your cycle path. Who has priority?

options.label: The car — it was there first
You — vehicles exiting driveways must yield to cycle path traffic
Neither — you should both stop

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Any vehicle exiting a private driveway, garage, or car park must yield to all traffic on the road or cycle path it is joining. They are entering the traffic flow — not the other way around.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 — vehicles exiting private properties yield to all road users.
feedback.tip: Exiting a driveway = you yield to everything. Every time.

prompt: You're on a priority road (yellow diamond). A pedestrian steps off the pavement onto the road ahead of you. Does your priority sign protect you?

options.label: Yes — you are on a priority road
No — priority road signs apply to vehicle junctions, not pedestrians
Only if the pedestrian is not at a crossing

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Priority road signs govern right of way between vehicles at junctions. They have no effect on your obligation toward pedestrians. You must always avoid hitting a pedestrian, regardless of what road you're on.
feedback.rule: Voorrangsweg signs apply at vehicle junctions only. Pedestrian rules apply everywhere.
feedback.tip: Priority road = beats cars at junctions. Never beats a pedestrian.

prompt: You approach a red inverted triangle sign (yield sign) at a junction. What are you required to do?

options.label: Come to a complete stop, then proceed when safe
Slow down and yield if traffic is present — no mandatory stop
Stop and wait for a green light
Flash your lights and proceed with caution

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A yield sign (inverted triangle) means slow down and give way if necessary — but you don't have to stop if the road is clear. A STOP sign (red octagon) requires you to come to a complete standstill regardless. These are different obligations.
feedback.rule: Yield sign = slow and give way if needed. STOP sign = mandatory full stop.
feedback.tip: Inverted triangle = yield if needed. Red octagon = full stop always.

prompt: You and another cyclist approach an unmarked crossing from exactly diagonal directions — neither is directly to the right of the other. What applies?

options.label: Both stop and whoever moves first goes
Right-before-left still applies — assess each other's relative position
Diagonal approaches are always a tie — both yield

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Right-before-left applies regardless of the angle. You need to assess whether the other cyclist is to your right or your left based on your direction of travel — not the abstract geometry of the crossing. The one whose relative right has the other approaching must yield.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 15 — right-before-left applies to relative positions, not angles.
feedback.tip: Not sure who's right of whom? Track your direction, then look right.

prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the Dutch junction priority hierarchy, from highest to lowest?

options.label: Emergency vehicles with lights and sirens
Trams
Vehicles on a priority road (yellow diamond)
Emergency vehicles, then trams, then priority road vehicles

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: The Dutch priority hierarchy runs: emergency vehicles (lights and sirens) first, then trams, then traffic on a priority road, then right-before-left as the default. Each level overrides the one below it.
feedback.rule: Priority hierarchy: emergency vehicles > trams > priority road > right-before-left.
feedback.tip: Blue lights > trams > yellow diamond > right-before-left. In that order.

prompt: You need to cross tram tracks that run diagonally across your path. What is the safest way to cross?

options.label: Speed up to minimise the time your wheel is on the rail
Cross at your natural travel angle — it's only a short contact point
Steer to cross the tracks as close to perpendicular as possible

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Crossing tram tracks at an angle allows your front wheel to slip into the groove and throw you off — it's one of the most common cycling crashes in Dutch cities. Steering to cross at 90° keeps your wheel rolling straight across the gap. Even a short detour to find a more perpendicular crossing is worth taking.
feedback.rule: Cross tram tracks as close to 90° as possible. Diagonal crossings allow wheels to catch in the rail groove.
feedback.tip: Square up to the tracks. Diagonal = wheel trap.

prompt: You're at a red light alongside a large truck. The truck is indicating right and you plan to go straight. The light turns green. What do you do?

options.label: Hold back and let the truck complete its turn before you move
Move forward — you're going straight, so you have priority over the turning truck
Accelerate past the front of the truck before it starts turning

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A truck turning right cannot see you alongside or behind the cab — the blind spot covers the entire side of the vehicle. Even if you have priority going straight, being legally right doesn't help if the driver doesn't see you. Hold back, let it go, then move.
feedback.rule: Never position yourself alongside a large vehicle that is indicating to turn. Their blind spot covers the full vehicle length.
feedback.tip: Truck + indicator = danger zone. Hold back, let it go, then move.

prompt: You're on a shared path used by both cyclists and pedestrians. Walkers ahead don't move after you ring your bell. What do you do?

options.label: Slow down and pass at a safe distance — they have equal right to use the path
Continue at speed — ringing your bell gives you the right to pass

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: On a shared path, pedestrians and cyclists have equal rights to the space. Ringing your bell is a courtesy warning, not a legal instruction for pedestrians to move. You must reduce your speed and pass at a distance that is safe for them.
feedback.rule: On shared paths, cyclists must adapt to pedestrians — not the other way around.
feedback.tip: Bell = warning, not an order. Shared path means shared space.

prompt: You're turning right. As you enter the turn, a pedestrian steps onto a zebra crossing on your intended path. What do you do?

options.label: Continue — you were already mid-turn when they stepped out
Ring your bell and proceed slowly
Stop and yield — turning traffic must give way to pedestrians on a crossing

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: Pedestrians on a zebra crossing have priority, and all turning traffic — including cyclists — must yield to them. Being mid-turn does not change this. The pedestrian stepped out legally; you must stop.
feedback.rule: RVV 1990 Art. 49a — all turning road users must yield to pedestrians on a zebra crossing.
feedback.tip: Turning + zebra crossing = pedestrian goes first. Always.

prompt: You're cycling past a row of parked cars. Where should you position yourself?

options.label: As close to the cars as possible to stay out of the traffic lane
At least 1 metre from the cars to stay outside the door zone

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A car door swings roughly 1 metre when fully opened. Riding closer than that puts you directly in its path with no reaction time. Riding 1 metre out feels exposed, but it is the correct — and safe — position.
feedback.rule: Cycle at least 1 metre from parked cars. The door zone is a common cause of serious cycling injuries.
feedback.tip: 1 metre from parked cars. Door zone = danger zone.

prompt: A triangular give-way sign reads 'Voorrang verlenen'. What does this require you to do?

options.label: Give way to traffic on the crossing road
Stop completely and wait for a clear gap
Sound your bell before entering the junction
You have priority — other traffic must yield to you

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Voorrang verlenen' means give way — you must yield to traffic on the crossing road. A full stop is not always required, but you must wait until it is safe to proceed. 'Verlenen' means to grant, so you are granting priority to others.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: voorrang = priority, verlenen = to grant. Voorrang verlenen = give way.
feedback.tip: Verlenen = to give. Voorrang verlenen = grant priority to others.

prompt: A Dutch cyclist warns you to watch for 'haaientanden' at the junction ahead. What should you look for?

options.label: A yellow diamond sign indicating a priority road
A red and white triangular give-way sign
White triangles painted on the road marking a yield obligation
Raised speed bumps across the cycle path

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Haaientanden' literally means shark teeth — the white triangles painted on the road surface. They always point toward the road user who must yield. If they point at you, you must give way to crossing traffic.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: haaien = sharks, tanden = teeth. Haaientanden = white yield triangles painted on the road.
feedback.tip: Haaientanden = shark teeth. Pointing at you = you yield.

prompt: You're cycling on a red road surface with 'FIETSSTRAAT' painted on it. A car behind you wants to pass. You're cycling at a comfortable pace. What applies?

options.label: Speed up to avoid slowing the car down
Hold your position — on a fietsstraat, cars are guests and may not overtake cyclists
Move aside — motor vehicles have priority to pass on any road

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: A fietsstraat is a cycle street where cyclists have full priority and cars are guests. Motor vehicles may use the road to access properties but must not overtake cyclists or cause them to change speed or direction. You set the pace.
feedback.rule: On a fietsstraat, motor vehicles may not overtake cyclists. Cyclists have full use of the road width.
feedback.tip: Fietsstraat = your street. Cars follow you, not the other way around.

prompt: A car is emerging from a driveway marked 'Uitrit'. You are cycling past on the adjacent cycle path. Who has priority?

options.label: The car — it is a vehicle using a recognised exit point
Neither — you must both slow and negotiate
Whoever reaches the conflict point first
You — vehicles leaving a uitrit must yield to all road and cycle path users

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Uitrit' means driveway exit. Vehicles leaving a uitrit must yield to all traffic on the public road or cycle path — including cyclists. This applies even if the car is already partially across the cycle path when you arrive.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: uit = out, rit = drive. Uitrit = driveway exit. Exiting vehicles always yield to passing traffic.
feedback.tip: Uitrit = their exit, your priority. They yield, always.

prompt: An older sign at a path entrance reads 'Rijwielpad'. What kind of path is this, and who may use it?

options.label: A mandatory cycle path — cyclists must use it, motor vehicles prohibited
A shared path — pedestrians and cyclists both welcome
An advisory cycle route — you may use the road instead
A path for all non-motorised vehicles including mopeds

feedback.title: Correct
feedback.body: 'Rijwielpad' is an older Dutch term for a mandatory cycle path — now more commonly called a fietspad. 'Rijwiel' is a formal word for bicycle, 'pad' means path. As a cyclist you must use it; motor vehicles are prohibited. You will still encounter this sign on older infrastructure.
feedback.rule: Vocabulary: rijwiel = bicycle (formal), pad = path. Rijwielpad = mandatory cycle path, same rules as fietspad.
feedback.tip: Rijwielpad = old word for fietspad. Mandatory for you, forbidden for cars.
